title: loadState 'corrupt' kind conflates four distinct failure modes
description: loadState collapses four very different failure conditions into the single 'corrupt' kind: (1) non-ENOENT readFile errors (EACCES, ENOTDIR), (2) unparseable JSON, (3) JSON-parseable-but-not-an-object/missing schema_version, and (4) JSON-with-correct-version-but-failed-shape-validation. The contract calls these out as deliberately distinct in spirit ('the contract refuses to conflate them'), and #1 in particular is asymmetric — it returns archivedTo: '' while the other three return a real archive path. A consumer that does `if (result.archivedTo)` can read those four cases as one when each really wants its own kind ('read-error' | 'corrupt-bytes' | 'corrupt-shape' | 'corrupt-schema'). The contract A6 already implicitly demands this distinction (read-error vs ENOENT) and the implementation only honors it via free-text in the message field, which downstream code cannot match on without regex-grepping a human string.
impact: Cycle 4's recovery flow (and any future telemetry) cannot pattern-match on 'is this a permission problem the user can fix' vs 'are these bytes salvageable' without reaching into the message string. The message-only distinction also drives the awkward `archivedTo: ''` empty-sentinel in the read-error branch — see R2-002.
recommendation: Either widen LoadStateResult with finer kinds ('read-error' | 'corrupt' | 'schema-mismatch' | 'absent') and let start.ts decide how to present them, or add a sub-field `corruptReason: 'read-error' | 'invalid-json' | 'invalid-shape'` to the corrupt arm. Drop the empty-string archivedTo sentinel either way.

title: Empty-string archivedTo sentinel breaks the discriminated-union contract
description: The LoadStateResult type at lines 17-21 declares the corrupt arm as `{ kind: 'corrupt'; archivedTo: string; message: string }` — implying every corrupt result has a usable archive path. The EACCES/ENOTDIR branch lies to that type by returning `archivedTo: ''`. start.ts then forwards that empty string into the StateWarning as if it were a real path (line 63), which the harness test T-077 happens not to catch because it checks `kind === 'state-corrupt'`. A consumer that opens the archive (cycle 4's recovery flow, or a logging hook) will silently fail. The type either has to admit `archivedTo?: string` (recommended; matches the schema-mismatch arm's shape) or this branch needs its own kind. Either way, an empty string masquerading as an absolute path is the wrong move.
impact: Type-level guarantee is false: 'corrupt' results no longer always carry an archive. Future code reading archivedTo as load-bearing will misbehave on the read-error branch.
recommendation: Make archivedTo optional in the corrupt arm (`archivedTo?: string`) OR introduce a separate 'read-error' kind. Drop the `''` sentinel.

title: registry.ts RegistryWarning.kind is still a loose `string`, defeating the discriminated-union benefit when state warnings join the array
description: Cycle 1's review (C003) flagged `RegistryWarning.kind: string` as a missed discriminated-union opportunity. Cycle 2 doubled down by introducing a *second* warning shape (StateWarning) at tools/start.ts:8-13 and joining them into a single `(RegistryWarning | StateWarning)[]` array. RegistryWarning's kind being `string` means the union effectively degrades to 'any string' — a consumer cannot exhaustively switch on warning kinds, and a typo in 'state-corrupt' anywhere in the codebase will not be caught by the compiler. The union also shows a parallel-shape problem: RegistryWarning has optional `path?` and `dir?`, StateWarning has optional `archivedTo?` and `foundVersion?`, and they both have `{ kind, message }`. They want to be unified.
impact: Two concerns: (a) the cycle 1 carry-forward (C003) didn't get addressed even though cycle 2 was a natural moment to fix it — the union literally needed to widen — and (b) the new StateWarning is shaped just like RegistryWarning with different optional fields. Long-term this is going to fork into N parallel warning interfaces (cycle 3 will add a preflight warning shape).
recommendation: Define a single `EngineWarning` discriminated union in a shared place (e.g. `mcp/server/src/warnings.ts`) keyed by literal `kind` values. Each kind gets its own narrow shape. registry.ts and state.ts both produce members of the union; tools/start.ts consumes the union without a structural sum.

title: StateWarning lives in tools/start.ts but is a domain shape, not a tool concern
description: The contract declares state.ts as 'pure I/O + classification', and yet the response-shape interface that maps loadState's outcomes onto the wire — StateWarning — lives in tools/start.ts. This is exactly the boundary the contract said state.ts 'does not know about' (line 11 of contract.md). That cleanly separates the producer side (state.ts emits LoadStateResult) but lands the consumer-facing warning shape inside the tool handler that happens to surface it first. Cycle 4 will add at least three more callers of saveState (selectPath/setPersonalization/nextSpot) and possibly more emitters. If any of *them* surface state warnings, they'll either re-import StateWarning from tools/start.ts (creating a backwards dependency from one tool into another) or duplicate the shape.
impact: A future tool that wants to surface state-corrupt or state-schema-mismatch (e.g. cycle 4's selectPath after a corruption-during-save) has to reach into another tool's file to grab the type. Tool files should be terminal consumers of types, not producers.
recommendation: Move StateWarning into a sibling of state.ts (e.g. mcp/server/src/state.ts itself, or a new mcp/server/src/warnings.ts as part of R2-003). The tool then imports it like any other domain type.

title: index.ts mixes library exports, tool registration, and bin entrypoint behind a process.argv guard
description: index.ts carries three concerns: (a) re-exporting SDK seams (McpServer, Client, InMemoryTransport) so the test harness has a single import path; (b) the registerTools(server) factory; (c) the stdio bin entrypoint guarded by a `process.argv[1] === fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)` check. The third is the leaky bit. The standard ESM way to split bin from library is to put the bin in its own file (`src/bin.ts`) listed under `package.json#bin`, and keep `index.ts` purely as the library surface. The argv-guard pattern works but it is fragile (symlinks, `node --import` loaders, and process managers can all break the equality check) and it forces every importer of the library — including the test harness — to load and parse 30+ extra lines of stdio bootstrap they will never use.
impact: Two failure modes. First, anyone consuming `index.ts` as a library (the harness already does, and cycle-4+ TS dApp will probably do) loads the SDK's stdio-transport module unnecessarily — small bundle hit. Second, if a future runner invokes `node --loader tsx src/index.ts` or similar, argv[1] may not equal the resolved file URL and the bin will silently never start. The argv guard also resists static analysis: tools that look at top-level effects don't see the conditional spawn.
recommendation: Extract the bin into `mcp/server/src/bin.ts` (or `cli.ts`); keep `index.ts` as the export surface only. Update `package.json#bin` and the `start`/`dev` scripts. The harness keeps importing from `index.ts` unchanged.

title: SDK re-export pattern in index.ts couples library to test-only seams
description: Lines 10-12 re-export McpServer, Client, and InMemoryTransport from the index module so the harness can resolve them through one import. The comment is honest about why — 'so the in-process harness can resolve all classes from a single import without needing @modelcontextprotocol/sdk installed at the workspace root.' That solves the workspace-vs-harness boundary problem (cycle 1 C018 carry-forward), but it does it by widening the *library's* public surface to include test infrastructure. McpServer is a server-side seam; Client and InMemoryTransport are *client-side* and only meaningful for in-process testing. A library user who imports `@sui-deepbook-course/mcp-server` and sees `Client` exported will reasonably wonder why the server package re-exports a client. The cleaner answer is to add `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` to the test root's dependencies (workspace root or a `tests/package.json`) so the harness imports directly from the SDK. That keeps the library boundary narrow.
impact: Cycle 4+ when this package is consumed externally, the apparent API surface includes Client + InMemoryTransport, which are not part of what `registerTools` does. Renaming or upgrading the SDK becomes a breaking change to consumers who never asked for those re-exports. Cosmetic but will accumulate.
recommendation: Add `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` to the workspace root's devDependencies (or the tests workspace) and have the harness import McpServer/Client/InMemoryTransport directly from the SDK. Keep `index.ts` exporting only `registerTools` and any genuinely public engine types.
evidence:
export { Client } from '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/index.js';
export { InMemoryTransport } from '@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/inMemory.js';

title: Naming asymmetry: archive helper takes _statePath that it never uses
description: archiveCorruptFile takes three parameters (stateDir, _statePath, content) but the underscore prefix on _statePath is the standard 'unused parameter' convention. The fact that the function takes a parameter it doesn't use is a smell: either the original design intended to do something with it (e.g. read the bytes from the path rather than receive them already-buffered) and was abandoned, or the parameter is leftover. Either way, callers are passing in a value that is silently discarded. This is the kind of thing reviewers in cycle 1 caught for `RegistryWarning.dir?` (C006).
impact: Cosmetic, but a future maintainer will have to read the body to discover the parameter is dead. Two callers (lines 49, 64, 85) all pass it; that's three call sites to update if/when the param is dropped.
recommendation: Drop the _statePath parameter. The bytes are already passed in via `content`; the function does not need the path.

title: Module-level archiveCounter creates hidden cross-test coupling
description: `let archiveCounter = 0;` is module-scoped and monotonically increases across every loadState call in the process lifetime. This works for the contract's same-millisecond-collision requirement, but it introduces hidden state that survives across distinct project roots and across distinct test cases. It also means archive filenames in a long-running process slowly drift away from a clean ISO timestamp ('state.corrupt-2026-04-28T12-00-00.000Z-47.json'). For a CLI tool that runs once per invocation, this is fine; for the in-process harness that boots a server and shuts it down per test, the counter becomes a leaky abstraction (test A's last archive number influences test B's archive number). Not load-bearing today, but the counter wants to be either (a) per-server-instance (move into the closure of a constructed StateAdapter) or (b) replaced with crypto.randomBytes(2).toString('hex') for the suffix.
impact: Tests are technically order-dependent: T-050's archives have suffix `-1` and `-2` on a clean run but `-47` and `-48` if other tests have run first. None of the assertions match against the counter value, but a future test that does (e.g. asserting suffix `-1` for the first corruption) will silently pass-or-fail based on test order.
recommendation: Replace `archiveCounter` with `crypto.randomBytes(3).toString('hex')` or `process.hrtime.bigint()` for collision avoidance — both are stateless and don't carry cross-call coupling.
evidence:
// Monotonic counter to ensure uniqueness within the same millisecond
let archiveCounter = 0;

title: saveState mixes async fsPromises with sync openSync/fsyncSync/closeSync inside an async function
description: saveState is `async`, awaits fsPromises.writeFile, then drops to synchronous fs.openSync / fs.fsyncSync / fs.closeSync to fsync the tmp file, then awaits fsPromises.rename. The contract calls for 'write-tmp-then-fsync-then-rename' atomicity, which the implementation satisfies — but mixing the async and sync APIs is a design smell: a slow fsync (the whole point of fsync) blocks the event loop, defeating the reason saveState is async in the first place. The Node guidance for this pattern is to use `fsPromises.open()` returning a FileHandle, then `await fileHandle.sync()` and `await fileHandle.close()`. That keeps the function uniformly async and avoids holding the event loop during the durability barrier.
impact: On a real filesystem with a slow fsync (network mounts, encrypted drives), saveState blocks the entire MCP server's event loop for the duration of the fsync. In cycle 4, when this runs from inside an MCP tool handler, that means no other tool can be answered concurrently. The contract's intent ('async I/O surface') is preserved at the type level but defeated at the runtime level.
recommendation: Replace the openSync/fsyncSync/closeSync block with `const fh = await fsPromises.open(tmpPath, 'r'); try { await fh.sync(); } finally { await fh.close(); }`.

title: schemas/state.ts validator returns the original input, not a normalized copy — Personalization and HistoryEntry shapes are unchecked
description: validateState confirms that `personalization` is an object and `history` is an array, then casts: `personalization: obj['personalization'] as Personalization` and `history: obj['history'] as HistoryEntry[]`. The cast is a lie — the validator never inspects the inner shape (e.g. that history elements have `ts: string` and `event: string`, or that personalization has `poll_interval_ms: number`). A consumer reading `state.history[0].ts` will get whatever was on disk, which the type system claims is a string. This deliberately-shallow check is consistent with the contract ('cycle 2 only enforces top-level shape and schema_version'), but it's worth flagging that the cast asserts more than the runtime checks. The path.ts validator at lines 41-48 went one level deeper for personalization_options; the state validator wants the same level of rigor for personalization and history. The shape mirrors path.ts/phases.ts only structurally; the depth-of-validation idiom is not preserved.
impact: If on-disk state.json has `personalization: { poll_interval_ms: 'three thousand' }`, validateState returns ok:true and the consumer crashes when it does arithmetic on the string. The corruption was real but classified as ok. Cycle 4's selectPath/nextSpot will be the first to dereference these inner fields.
recommendation: Either (a) tighten validateState to check `obj.personalization.poll_interval_ms` is a number, `obj.history[i].ts/event` are strings (matches path.ts depth); or (b) explicitly document the shallow-only contract and stop typing the result as Personalization/HistoryEntry — return them as `Record<string, unknown>` and `unknown[]` until cycle 4 deepens the check.
evidence:
personalization: obj['personalization'] as Personalization,
      cursor: {
        phase_id: cursor['phase_id'] as string,
        spot_id: cursor['spot_id'] as string,
      },
      ladder: obj['ladder'] as Record<string, LadderRung>,
      history: obj['history'] as HistoryEntry[],
